nativeRange.habitat: Marshy and aquatic environments — typically grows along the margins of streams, ponds, and seasonally flooded wetlands, transitioning between submerged and emergent forms

decorPreferences: background or tall midground planting area
stems spaced 2-5 cm apart in groups
tolerates plain gravel or sand but benefits from nutrient-rich substrate
can also be grown as a floating plant

avoidWith: plant-eating fish such as goldfish, silver dollars, and large herbivorous cichlids

summary: Water Wisteria (Hygrophila difformis) is a fast-growing stem plant from the Indian subcontinent, widely recommended for beginners. Submerged leaves are lacy and deeply lobed; emergent leaves are broad and oval. It tolerates a wide range of water conditions, grows without added CO2, and works equally well as a background plant or a trimmed midground feature.
careNotes: Hygrophila difformis is one of the most widely recommended beginner stem plants. Growth is fast — up to several cm per day once converted to submerged form — and the plant readily takes up ammonium and nitrate, helping to suppress algae in newly planted tanks. Medium to high lighting is recommended; under low light, leaves revert to a simpler shape and the plant may melt during establishment. The species exhibits striking heterophylly — submerged foliage is lacy and deeply pinnate, while emergent leaves are broad, oval, and unlobed — so newly purchased emergent-grown stock often melts back its initial leaves before producing aquatic foliage, a process that can take a couple of weeks to a couple of months. Plant stems roughly 2-5 cm apart in groups; nutrient-rich substrate and an all-in-one liquid fertilizer encourage best growth, and iron supplementation helps prevent yellowing. The plant also grows happily floating at the surface. Best used as a background plant or, with regular trimming, as a tall, bushy midground feature. A tank of at least 38 L (10 gal) is generally suggested to give stems room to reach their full height of 20-50 cm.
breedingNotes: Propagation is trivial via stem cuttings: trim the top 8-15 cm of a healthy stem, remove the lowest pair of leaves, and replant directly into the substrate. New roots form quickly. Lateral side shoots from trimmed stems can be cut and replanted the same way. Regular topping is the standard way to maintain a dense stand and rejuvenate stems whose bases have begun to lose leaves.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op
primary.notes: Primary source. Provides adult size (up to 51 cm tall, 25 cm wide), growth rate (1-8 cm/day), medium-to-high lighting, propagation by topping, planting spacing, melting-during-conversion notes, and India-to-Thailand native range estimate.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Acanthaceae, Lamiales), native range (Bangladesh, Bhutan, India, Nepal), heterophylly between submerged and emergent leaves, adult size (20-50 cm tall, 15-25 cm wide), Least Concern conservation status, and image candidates.

additional.name: Flowgrow Aquatic Plant Database
additional.notes: Used for full tolerance ranges (temperature 18-30°C, pH 6-7.5, kH 2-21°dKH), CO2 10-40 mg/l, medium-to-high light, fast growth, easy difficulty, and notes on finer pinnate leaves under strong light plus benefit of iron supplementation.
